questions.question:
An algorithm has to store several keys generated by an adversary in a hash table. The adversary is malicious who tries to maximize the number of collisions. Let n be the number of keys, m be the number of slots in the hash table, and n < m. Which one of the following is the best hashing strategy to counteract the adversary?

A. Division method, i.e., use the hash function h(k) = k mod m
B. Multiplication method, i.e., use the hash function h(k) = floor(m * (k*A - floor(k*A))), where A is a carefully chosen constant
C. Universal hashing method
D. If m is a prime number, use Division method. Otherwise, use Multiplication method.
questions.to_find: Best hashing strategy to counteract a malicious adversary trying to maximize collisions

understand.plain: A hash table is being built, but an adversary knows the hash function and deliberately picks keys that all hash to the same slot, causing maximum collisions. We need a strategy that the adversary cannot predict and exploit. Universal hashing randomly selects a hash function from a family, so the adversary cannot know which function will be used.

keywords.explain: Keys specifically chosen by a malicious party to cause worst-case behavior (all keys colliding) in a deterministic hash function.
keywords.example: If h(k) = k mod 10, an adversary can insert 10, 20, 30, ... to always collide at slot 0.

keywords.explain: A randomized strategy where the hash function is chosen uniformly at random from a family of functions. No fixed set of keys can cause many collisions with high probability because the adversary does not know which function was selected.
keywords.example: Family h_{a,b}(k) = ((a*k + b) mod p) mod m for random a, b ensures expected O(1) collisions regardless of input.

keywords.explain: h(k) = k mod m. Deterministic and predictable; an adversary who knows m can choose keys that all map to the same slot.
keywords.example: With m=10, keys 10, 20, 30 all map to slot 0.

understand.visual_alt: A hash table with slots 0 to m-1. In a deterministic scheme, the adversary sends all keys to one slot. Universal hashing randomizes the function so no fixed set of keys can always collide.

given.aim: Choose a hashing strategy that performs well even when input keys are chosen adversarially to maximize collisions.

terms.meaning: Number of keys to be inserted
Number of slots in the hash table
terms.connects: n < m ensures load factor < 1, so collisions are avoidable in principle

terms.meaning: Knows the hash function and picks keys to maximize collisions
terms.example: Knows h(k)=k mod m and picks multiples of m
terms.connects: Makes deterministic hash functions vulnerable; only randomness defeats the adversary

given.plan: Analyze each option: Division method and Multiplication method are both deterministic — an adversary who knows m (and the constant A) can craft keys that all map to the same slot. Universal hashing picks a random function from a family at runtime, so even if the adversary knows the family, they cannot predict which function is used, guaranteeing expected O(n/m) collisions per slot regardless of input.

steps.title: Evaluate deterministic methods (A, B, D)
steps.formula_raw: h(k) = k mod m  OR  h(k) = floor(m*(k*A - floor(k*A)))
steps.apply: Both Division and Multiplication methods produce a fixed mapping from key to slot. If the adversary knows m (and A for multiplication), they can compute which keys collide and deliberately choose those keys. Option D simply switches between them based on primality of m, but both remain deterministic and exploitable.
steps.note: A deterministic hash function always has an adversarial input that causes Theta(n) collisions on any single slot.

steps.title: Evaluate Universal hashing (C)
steps.formula_raw: For any two distinct keys x, y: Pr[h(x) = h(y)] <= 1/m
steps.apply: In universal hashing, the hash function h is chosen uniformly at random from a family H at the start of the algorithm (not visible to the adversary before key selection). Even if the adversary knows the entire family H, they cannot predict which h will be chosen. By definition, for any fixed pair of keys x != y, the probability that h(x) = h(y) is at most 1/m. Thus the expected number of collisions for any fixed set of n keys is at most C(n,2)/m = O(n^2/m), which is O(n) when m = Omega(n). This bound holds regardless of how adversarially the keys were chosen.
steps.note: Randomness in the algorithm selection is the key defense: the adversary must commit to keys before the random hash function is revealed.

solution.result: C. Universal hashing method — it randomizes the choice of hash function so no adversary can force worst-case collisions.

questions.formula_note: Universal hashing guarantee: for any two distinct keys x, y, Pr[h(x)=h(y)] <= 1/m over random choice of h from a universal family H.

questions.question:
A hash table contains 10 buckets and uses linear probing to resolve collisions. The key values are integers and the hash function used is h(k) = k mod 10. If the values 43, 133, 62, 133, 142 are inserted in the table, in what location would the key value 142 be inserted?

A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 6
questions.to_find: The slot/bucket index where key 142 is inserted using linear probing

understand.plain: We have a hash table with 10 slots (0 to 9). We insert keys one by one using h(k) = k mod 10 and linear probing (if a slot is taken, try the next slot, wrapping around if needed). We need to trace all insertions in order and find where 142 ends up.

keywords.explain: Collision resolution where if slot h(k) is occupied, we try h(k)+1, h(k)+2, ... (mod m) until an empty slot is found.
keywords.example: h(k)=3, slot 3 full -> try slot 4 -> if full try slot 5, etc.

keywords.explain: Maps any integer key to a slot 0-9 by taking the last digit (remainder when divided by 10).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' slot 6</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hash table with 10 slots. Slot 2: 62, Slot 3: 43, Slot 4: first 133, Slot 5: second 133, Slot 6: 142 (highlighted).

given.aim: Trace insertion of keys 43, 133, 62, 133, 142 into a 10-slot hash table with linear probing and determine final position of 142.

terms.meaning: Hash function mapping key k to slot 0-9
terms.connects: Determines starting slot for each key before probing

terms.meaning: If slot (h(k)+i) mod 10 is occupied, try i=1,2,3,... until empty slot found
terms.example: Key at slot 3 full -> try 4 -> if full try 5
terms.connects: Determines final resting slot when collisions occur

terms.example: State of table changes with each insertion
terms.connects: Earlier insertions occupy slots, forcing later keys to probe further

given.plan: Insert each key in the given order, applying linear probing to find the first empty slot. Track which slots are occupied after each insertion, then trace where 142 finally lands.

steps.title: Insert 43, 133 (first), 62
steps.apply:
Insert 43: h(43)=3, slot 3 empty -> place at 3. Occupied: {3}.
Insert 133 (1st): h(133)=3, slot 3 occupied -> probe slot 4, empty -> place at 4. Occupied: {3,4}.
Insert 62: h(62)=2, slot 2 empty -> place at 2. Occupied: {2,3,4}.
steps.note: Two copies of key 133 appear in the input; both are inserted (duplicates allowed in this problem).

steps.title: Insert 133 (second), then 142
steps.apply:
Insert 133 (2nd): h(133)=3, slot 3 occupied -> slot 4 occupied -> slot 5 empty -> place at 5. Occupied: {2,3,4,5}.
Insert 142: h(142)=2, slot 2 occupied -> slot 3 occupied -> slot 4 occupied -> slot 5 occupied -> slot 6 empty -> place at 6.
steps.note: 142 must probe 4 times past its home slot because slots 2,3,4,5 are all taken by previous insertions.

solution.result: Key 142 is inserted at slot 6. Answer: D. 6

questions.formula_note: Linear probing: next probe position = (home + i) mod m for i = 1, 2, ... until an empty slot is found.

questions.question:
A language uses an alphabet of six letters, {a, b, c, d, e, f}. The relative frequency of use of each letter of the alphabet in the language is as given below:

Letter | Relative Frequency
a      | 0.19
b      | 0.05
c      | 0.17
d      | 0.08
e      | 0.40
f      | 0.11

Design a prefix binary code for the language which would minimize the average length of the encoded words of the language. What is the minimum average code length (in bits per symbol)?
questions.to_find: Minimum average code length (in bits per symbol) using Huffman coding for the given letter frequencies

understand.plain: Huffman coding builds an optimal prefix-free binary code. Letters that appear more often get shorter codes. We combine the two least frequent letters at each step until one tree remains, then read off code lengths. The average code length is the weighted sum of (frequency * code length) for all letters.

keywords.explain: A greedy algorithm that builds a binary tree by repeatedly merging the two nodes with lowest frequency. More frequent symbols get shorter codes.
keywords.example: If e has frequency 0.40, it likely gets a 1-bit code (e.g., '0').

keywords.explain: Sum over all symbols of (frequency * number of bits in that symbol's code). Huffman coding minimizes this value.
keywords.example: If e=0.40 gets 1 bit and a=0.19 gets 3 bits, they contribute 0.40 + 0.57 = 0.97 bits.

understand.visual_alt: Huffman tree: root=1.00. Left subtree (0): internal node 0.60. Right child of root (1): e=0.40. Left of 0.60 (0): internal 0.24. Right of 0.60 (1): internal 0.36. Under 0.24: f=100 (left=0), internal 0.13=101 which splits into b=1010, d=1011. Under 0.36: c=110 (left=0), a=111 (right=1).

given.aim: Build a Huffman code for letters a-f with given frequencies to minimize average code length.

terms.example: e is most frequent (0.40), b is least frequent (0.05)
terms.connects: More frequent letters get shorter codes in Huffman tree

terms.meaning: Repeatedly merge the two nodes with smallest frequencies to form a new internal node
terms.example: Merge b(0.05) and d(0.08) -> new node 0.13

terms.meaning: Depth of a leaf in the Huffman tree equals the number of bits in its code
terms.example: e at depth 1 gets a 1-bit code
terms.connects: Used to compute average code length = sum(freq_i * depth_i)

given.plan: Sort letters by frequency, repeatedly merge the two with lowest frequency, build the Huffman tree, read off depths, compute average code length = sum over all letters of (frequency * code length).

steps.title: Build the Huffman tree by repeated merging
steps.formula_raw: Merge two minimum-frequency nodes at each step
steps.note: The exact code assignment (which branch is 0 and which is 1) does not affect the average length, only the depths matter.

steps.title: Read code lengths and compute average
steps.apply:
Depths from the tree:
e: depth 1, code length = 1
f: depth 3, code length = 3
b: depth 4, code length = 4
d: depth 4, code length = 4
c: depth 3, code length = 3
a: depth 3, code length = 3

Average = 0.40*1 + 0.11*3 + 0.05*4 + 0.08*4 + 0.17*3 + 0.19*3
        = 0.40 + 0.33 + 0.20 + 0.32 + 0.51 + 0.57
        = 2.33 bits per symbol
steps.note: This is the theoretical minimum achievable by any prefix-free code for this probability distribution, equal to what Huffman achieves.

solution.result: Minimum average code length = 2.33 bits per symbol.

questions.formula_note: Huffman coding greedy algorithm: repeatedly merge two minimum-frequency nodes; average length = sum(freq_i * depth_i) over all symbols.

questions.question:
Suppose the letters a, b, c, d, e, f have probabilities 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32, 1/32 respectively. Which of the following is the Huffman code for the letters a, b, c, d, e, f?

A. 0, 10, 110, 1110, 11110, 11111
B. 11, 10, 01, 001, 0001, 0000
C. 11, 10, 01, 001, 0001, 0000
D. 0, 10, 110, 1110, 11110, 11111
questions.to_find: The correct Huffman code assignment for letters a, b, c, d, e, f with the given probabilities

understand.plain: The probabilities 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32, 1/32 are in geometric sequence (each half the previous, with e and f equal). Huffman coding assigns shorter codes to more probable letters. Letter 'a' with probability 1/2 should get the shortest code (1 bit), 'b' gets 2 bits, 'c' gets 3 bits, 'd' gets 4 bits, and 'e','f' (tied) each get 5 bits.

keywords.explain: Probabilities halve at each step: 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, ... This structure means Huffman coding produces a perfectly balanced right-leaning chain, where each symbol gets one more bit than the previous.
No codeword is a prefix of another. Ensures unambiguous decoding.
keywords.example: If 'a'=0, no other code can start with 0, so 'b'=10, 'c'=110, etc.

mut'>1</text><circle cx='345' cy='198' r='14' class='ok' style='stroke:#1D9E75'/><text x='345' y='204' class='sub' style='fill:#fff' text-anchor='middle'>c</text><circle cx='440' cy='198' r='14' class='bg'/><text x='440' y='204' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>...</text><text x='240' y='240' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Chain continues: d=1110, e=11110, f=11111</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Huffman tree for geometric probabilities: root splits into a (code=0) on left and an internal node on right. The right subtree chains down: b=10, then internal, then c=110, then internal, then d=1110, then internal, then e=11110 and f=11111.

given.aim: Find the Huffman code for a, b, c, d, e, f with probabilities 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32, 1/32.

terms.example: a is 16x more probable than e or f
terms.connects: Determines which symbols get shorter codes

terms.meaning: Merge two minimum-probability nodes at each step
terms.connects: Builds the tree that determines code lengths

terms.meaning: a=1 bit, b=2 bits, c=3 bits, d=4 bits, e=5 bits, f=5 bits
terms.example: More probable symbol = smaller depth = fewer bits

given.plan: Build the Huffman tree by merging pairs of minimum-frequency nodes. For these geometric probabilities, the tree is a right-skewed chain. Read off the codes for each letter from root to leaf.

steps.title: Build Huffman tree by merging
steps.formula_raw: Merge two nodes with minimum probability
steps.note: Each merge produces a right-leaning chain structure because of the geometric probability distribution.

steps.title: Assign codes (left branch = 0, right branch = 1)
steps.formula_raw: Code = path from root to leaf (0 for left, 1 for right)
steps.apply:
Root splits: left->a, right->BCDEF
a = 0 (depth 1)
BCDEF splits: left->b, right->CDEF
b = 10 (depth 2)
CDEF splits: left->c, right->DEF
c = 110 (depth 3)
DEF splits: left->d, right->EF
d = 1110 (depth 4)
EF splits: left->e, right->f
e = 11110 (depth 5)
f = 11111 (depth 5)

Codes: a=0, b=10, c=110, d=1110, e=11110, f=11111

questions.formula_note: Huffman coding for geometric probabilities produces a right-skewed chain tree; code for the k-th symbol (sorted by decreasing probability) has length k bits (last two symbols share the same length).
